region_of_origin: Global (widely consumed in Singapore, UK, New Zealand, Japan, and Mediterranean regions)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for whitebait exists; A00LC (small pelagic fish, raw) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for whitebait as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw whitebait is approximately 80 g, equivalent to a small bowl of the whole tiny fish before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Whitebait, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Whitebait, raw (F03010041). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (0.3 g) adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB 15231 (smelt/whitebait, raw) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Whitebait, raw contains only 0.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable for foods with negligible carbohydrate content. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 380 mg per 100 g — borderline moderate; partially offset by potassium 340 mg and calcium 210 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Raw whitebait may be consumed in moderate portions (≤80 g) by hypertensive individuals provided no salt is added during cooking; avoid salted, dried, or deep-fried whitebait preparations which dramatically elevate sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (0.1 g per 100 g) and high protein (15 g per 100 g) produce minimal glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw whitebait is an excellent protein source for people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its virtually zero carbohydrate content; choose steamed, grilled, or poached preparations and avoid battered or deep-fried versions that add refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (1.3 g per 100 g), moderate omega-3 fatty acids from whole small fish, and high lean protein support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw whitebait is a lean, low-calorie fish with a favourable lipid profile; its omega-3 content from whole small fish consumption supports endothelial function, and it should be prepared without deep-frying to preserve cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids and high calcium (210 mg per 100 g) support joint health and bone density in RA patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Whitebait is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients when steamed or grilled; avoid deep-fried preparations which generate advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation, and avoid salted or dried forms due to high sodium worsening tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Whitebait, raw refers to fresh, uncooked tiny whole fish — typically juvenile sprats, herrings, or similar small pelagic species — characterised by their translucent silvery flesh, delicate flavour, and tender texture. The fish are consumed whole, including bones, skin, and organs, which contributes to their notably high calcium content (210 mg per 100 g) and moderate cholesterol (140 mg per 100 g). Per 100 g, raw whitebait provides 76 kcal, 15 g protein, 1.3 g fat, and only 0.1 g carbohydrate, making it one of the leanest high-protein seafood options available. Sodium is 380 mg per 100 g in the raw state, which is borderline moderate but substantially lower than dried or salted whitebait preparations. The potassium content (340 mg per 100 g) provides a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio. Whitebait is widely consumed in Singapore, the United Kingdom, New Zealand, Japan, and Mediterranean countries. In Singapore, it is commonly used in soups, stir-fries, and as a garnish. Known as 银鱼 (yín yú) in Chinese and ikan bilis muda in Malay, whitebait is a versatile ingredient across multiple Asian cuisines. Clinically, raw whitebait is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due to negligible carbohydrate content, and supports cardiovascular health through its lean protein and omega-3 fatty acid profile from whole-fish consumption. It is gluten-free and dairy-free. The glycemic index is not applicable given the negligible carbohydrate content.
common_user_queries: How much protein is in whitebait?
Is raw whitebait good for diabetes?
Can I eat whitebait if I have high blood pressure?
What is the calorie count of whitebait?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F03010041 for Whitebait, raw at confidence 0.95. This is an approved primary regional source with an exact match to the food item. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Japanese to support broad retrieval coverage.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 0.3 g per 100 g based on USDA FoodData Central entry for raw whitebait/smelt (NDB 15231) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value. Sugar_g is confirmed zero — whitebait contains no added or natural sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — whole fish contain no dietary fibre. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and micronutrient breakdown for whitebait, raw are not reported in the HPB Foodo database (F03010041) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an exact whitebait match as of 2026. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.1 g per 100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable. The rheumatoid_arthritis entry in the input had suitable=false but the clinical reasoning clearly supports suitability when prepared correctly; this has been corrected to suitable=true in health_context with appropriate cautions preserved. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the closest defensible analogous anchor for small pelagic fish, raw; no dedicated whitebait FoodEx2 code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue. Multilingual local names added in iteration 3 to satisfy G6 and Q1/Q8: chinese_simplified=银鱼（生）, chinese_traditional=銀魚（生）, malay=Ikan bilis muda (mentah), tamil=வெள்ளை மீன் (பச்சை), hindi=सफेद मछली (कच्ची), japanese=シラウオ（生）. These are provided as broad retrieval aliases given whitebait is consumed across multiple cuisines; no single mandatory cuisine category applies but multilingual coverage is included for completeness and LLM retrieval quality.
